attributes.stem:
A 22-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-month history of distressing perceptual changes that began after a period of intense academic stress. She describes feeling disconnected from her environment and that everything seems strange and unfamiliar. She reports that these sensations are constant but worsen when she is anxious. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She denies using recreational drugs and has no family history of mental illness.

On examination, she appears anxious but is oriented to time, place, and person. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Her mental state examination reveals clear consciousness, coherent speech, and no evidence of thought disorder or perceptual disturbances. She maintains insight that these experiences are not real.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following best describes this patient's predominant symptom?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic symptoms of derealisation, characterised by a persistent or recurrent experience of unreality and detachment from one's surroundings. The description of feeling disconnected from her environment with maintained insight is typical. Derealisation often occurs in the context of anxiety or stress, as seen in this case.

Depersonalisation involves feeling detached from oneself or one's mental processes, as if being an outside observer of one's thoughts, feelings, sensations and actions. While it often co-occurs with derealisation, this patient's symptoms primarily relate to her external environment rather than her sense of self.

Jamais vu is the experience of feeling that a familiar situation or place is completely new and unfamiliar. While this shares some features with derealisation, it is typically brief and episodic rather than persistent.

Micropsia is a specific type of perceptual distortion where objects appear smaller than they actually are. This differs from the patient's more generalised sense of environmental unreality.

Autoscopy is the experience of seeing oneself from an external perspective, like an out-of-body experience. This differs from the patient's symptoms which relate to her perception of the external environment.

Derealisation can occur independently or as part of depersonalisation-derealisation disorder. It is important to note that unlike psychotic disorders, reality testing remains intact - patients recognise that their altered perceptions are not real. Treatment typically involves addressing underlying anxiety and may include psychological therapies and/or medication.
learningPoints: Derealisation is characterised by feelings of unreality or detachment from one's surroundings, as if viewing the world through a veil or glass wall.
Derealisation commonly co-occurs with depersonalisation but can occur independently.
Derealisation symptoms are often triggered or worsened by stress, anxiety, or trauma.
references: Sadock BJ, Sadock VA, Ruiz P. Kaplan and Sadock's comprehensive textbook of psychiatry. lippincott Williams & wilkins; 2017 May 11.

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
